sources.title: fetch-pack: unexpected disconnect while reading sideband packet (shorebird #2397)
sources.note: Confirms the error is a transient network/transfer interruption; recommends wrapping git clone in retry logic that catches this error and tries again.

sources.title: git error fetch-pack unexpected disconnect inside container (microsoft/Windows-Containers #320)
sources.note: Same sideband-disconnect error reproduced in containerized environments; root cause is interrupted pack transfer.

sources.title: Fetch-pack: unexpected disconnect while reading sideband packet (Gitea forum)
sources.note: Server/proxy and large-transfer related; suggests http.postBuffer and compression workarounds.

sources.title: Little Git Tricks: Fix Unexpected Disconnect on Sideband Packet
sources.note: Workarounds: update git, increase http.postBuffer, disable core.compression, switch protocol, retry.

sources.title: How can I resolve fetch-pack: unexpected disconnect (Atlassian community)
sources.note: Network/packet-loss during large repo transfers; transient and retryable.

sources.title: gh repo set-default --view returns exit code 0 upon failure (cli/cli #9398)
sources.note: Documents a broader GitHub CLI pattern of returning exit code 0 on failure, reinforcing that the gh exit code cannot be trusted as the sole success signal.

key_findings: The error 'fetch-pack: unexpected disconnect while reading sideband packet' is universally classified as a transient network / interrupted-transfer failure, NOT a permanent error.
The community-recommended fix is exactly retry-with-backoff plus, optionally, http.postBuffer / core.compression tuning.
GitHub CLI (gh) is known to return exit code 0 in failure scenarios, so an exit code of 0 from `gh repo clone` must be corroborated by verifying that a real .git working tree was produced.
